title: Food Security and Water Resources
data_note: Statistics compiled from publicly available sources. Web verification was unavailable at compilation time; URLs point to known authoritative publication pages. Users should verify current figures at listed sources.

source.name: Taiwan Council of Agriculture (COA) / Food Supply and Utilization Annual Report

statistics.context: Taiwan's food self-sufficiency ratio on a calorie basis has hovered around 30-34% for the past decade. On a value basis it is higher (~60-65%) because Taiwan produces high-value fruits, vegetables, and aquaculture products while importing lower-value bulk commodities (grains, soybeans, wheat). This is one of the lowest self-sufficiency rates among developed economies.
statistics.verification_note: COA publishes annual food self-sufficiency statistics; calorie-basis ratio consistently in 30-34% range

statistics.context: The value-basis self-sufficiency is significantly higher than the calorie basis because Taiwan has competitive domestic production of fruits (mangoes, pineapples, guava), vegetables, seafood/aquaculture, pork, poultry, and rice. However, virtually all wheat, corn, soybeans, and feed grains are imported.
statistics.verification_note: COA annual food balance sheet; value-basis ratio typically 60-65%

statistics.context: Rice is the one major staple where Taiwan achieves self-sufficiency, producing slightly more than domestic demand. However, per-capita rice consumption has declined steadily (from ~100 kg/year in the 1980s to ~43 kg/year currently), and dietary patterns have shifted toward wheat-based products (bread, noodles), which are almost entirely imported.
statistics.verification_note: COA rice production and consumption data; surplus is small and declining with dietary shifts

source.name: Taiwan Council of Agriculture / Customs Administration (MOF)

statistics.context: Taiwan imports virtually 100% of its wheat, approximately 1.2-1.4 million metric tons annually. Primary sources are the United States (~50%), Australia (~25%), and Canada (~15%). Wheat is critical for bread, noodles, and processed foods that form an increasing share of the Taiwanese diet.
statistics.verification_note: Trade data from MOF Customs Administration and COA agricultural trade statistics

source.name: Taiwan Council of Agriculture / USDA Foreign Agricultural Service (FAS)

statistics.context: Taiwan imports approximately 2.5-2.7 million metric tons of soybeans annually, primarily from Brazil (~50%), the United States (~35%), and Argentina. Soybeans are used for tofu, soy sauce, cooking oil, and livestock feed. Domestic soybean production covers less than 3% of demand.
statistics.verification_note: USDA FAS Taiwan commodity reports and COA import data

source.name: USDA Foreign Agricultural Service / Taiwan COA

statistics.context: Taiwan imports approximately 4-5 million metric tons of corn annually, primarily for livestock feed. Major suppliers are the United States, Brazil, and Argentina. Domestic corn production is negligible. Disruption of corn imports would rapidly affect the livestock industry (pork, poultry, eggs).
statistics.verification_note: USDA FAS commodity reports for Taiwan

source.name: Water Resources Agency (WRA), Ministry of Economic Affairs

statistics.context: Taiwan has approximately 95 reservoirs with a total designed storage capacity of approximately 2.09 billion cubic meters. However, effective capacity has been reduced to approximately 1.7-1.8 billion cubic meters due to decades of sedimentation. Major reservoirs include Shihmen (Taoyuan), Zengwen (Tainan), and Feitsui (Taipei). Siltation is a chronic problem exacerbated by typhoon-driven erosion.
statistics.verification_note: WRA reservoir database; effective capacity lower than designed capacity due to sedimentation

source.name: Central Weather Administration (CWA, formerly CWB), Taiwan

statistics.context: Taiwan receives approximately 2,500 mm of rainfall annually, approximately 2.5 times the global average. However, due to steep terrain and short rivers, approximately 70-80% of rainfall runs off rapidly to the ocean. Rainfall is also highly seasonal (May-October wet season) and geographically uneven (mountainous east receives far more than the populated western plain). Climate change is making rainfall patterns more extreme.
statistics.verification_note: CWA long-term climate statistics; figure is island-wide average

statistics.metric: Taiwan water usage by sector - Agriculture
statistics.context: Agriculture (primarily rice paddy irrigation) accounts for approximately 70% of Taiwan's total water consumption. Industrial use accounts for approximately 10%, and domestic/municipal use approximately 20%. During droughts, agricultural water is typically the first to be rationed to maintain industrial and domestic supply.
statistics.verification_note: WRA publishes annual water resource utilization statistics

statistics.metric: Taiwan water usage by sector - Industrial
statistics.context: Industrial water use is approximately 10% of total consumption but is highly concentrated in semiconductor manufacturing regions (Hsinchu, Taichung, Tainan). During the 2021 drought, water rationing threatened semiconductor production, prompting emergency measures including water trucking to science parks.
statistics.verification_note: WRA water resource statistics; industrial share has been gradually increasing

statistics.metric: Taiwan water usage by sector - Domestic/Municipal
statistics.context: Domestic and municipal water use accounts for approximately 20% of total water consumption, serving Taiwan's 23.5 million population. Taiwan Water Corporation manages most municipal water supply.

TSMC consumed approximately 80 million metric tons of water in 2023, averaging roughly 220,000 metric tons per day. TSMC has invested heavily in water recycling (achieving approximately 96% recycling rate for process water) and reclaimed water sources. Advanced process nodes require ultra-pure water in increasing quantities. During the 2021 drought, TSMC trucked in water to maintain production.
statistics.verification_note: TSMC ESG/CSR reports publish annual water consumption data; daily figure calculated from annual total

statistics.context: TSMC achieves an industry-leading process water recycling rate of over 96%. Despite this high efficiency, total water demand continues to grow as new fabs come online. TSMC is investing in reclaimed water treatment plants near its major fab clusters.
statistics.verification_note: From TSMC 2023 ESG Report sustainability metrics

statistics.metric: Taiwan total annual water resource availability
statistics.unit: billion cubic meters (annual renewable water resources)
statistics.context: Taiwan's total renewable freshwater resources are approximately 17-18 billion cubic meters annually. Of this, approximately 15-20% is effectively captured and utilized. The per-capita water availability is approximately 3,900 cubic meters/year/person, close to the UN threshold for water-stressed nations (~4,000).
statistics.verification_note: FAO AQUASTAT and WRA data; utilization rate limited by runoff, topography, and infrastructure

source.name: Taiwan Council of Agriculture / Agriculture and Food Agency (AFA)

statistics.context: The government maintains a strategic rice reserve of approximately 300,000-350,000 metric tons, equivalent to roughly 3 months of domestic rice consumption. For wheat and other imported grains, commercial inventories typically provide only 1-2 months of supply. The total food reserve situation is precarious given the ~70% import dependency.
statistics.verification_note: AFA and COA public grain stock data; private sector grain inventories are estimated
